questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a 15-year history of poorly controlled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and slurred speech. CT of the head reveals a small, deep infarct in the left basal ganglia. Which of the following pathologic processes most likely underlies this patient's cerebrovascular event?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rupture of a berry aneurysm at the circle of Willis bifurcation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Embolism from an atherosclerotic plaque in the internal carotid artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lipohyalinosis of small penetrating arteries with subsequent vessel occlusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis of large cortical arteries with hemorrhagic conversion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amyloid angiopathy of leptomeningeal vessels with secondary thrombosis

explanation.summary: Chronic hypertension causes lipohyalinosis of small penetrating arteries, leading to vessel occlusion and lacunar infarcts in deep brain structures such as the basal ganglia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Persistent high blood pressure damages small blood vessels through oxidative stress and endothelial injury. Small arteries develop lipohyalinosis, a form of wall thickening associated with hypertension, leading to vessel occlusion with microinfarction of the surrounding brain (lacunar strokes). A small deep infarct in the basal ganglia is the classic presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Berry aneurysm rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage, not a lacunar infarct. Berry aneurysms are congenital and typically present at arterial bifurcations, not in small penetrating arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Large vessel atherosclerotic emboli typically cause larger cortical infarcts involving wedge-shaped territories, not small deep lacunar infarcts in the basal ganglia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrinoid necrosis is associated with malignant hypertension and affects arterioles acutely rather than causing the chronic small vessel disease that leads to lacunar infarcts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amyloid angiopathy preferentially affects cortical and leptomeningeal vessels and is associated with lobar hemorrhages in elderly patients, not deep lacunar infarcts.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman presents to an urgent care clinic with a blood pressure of 192/126 mm Hg. She reports mild intermittent headaches over the past week but denies chest pain, visual changes, or shortness of breath. Physical examination and point-of-care laboratory studies are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate hospitalization for intravenous antihypertensive therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral antihypertensive medication with close outpatient follow-up

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lifestyle modification alone with reassessment in 3 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Emergent CT angiography to evaluate for aortic dissection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Observation for 6 hours with repeat blood pressure measurement

explanation.summary: This patient has severe asymptomatic hypertension (formerly hypertensive urgency) with BP ≥180/120 mm Hg but no evidence of end-organ damage, which is managed with oral antihypertensives and close outpatient monitoring.
explanation.whyCorrect: Severe asymptomatic hypertension is defined as systolic BP ≥180 mm Hg and/or diastolic BP ≥120 mm Hg without signs of acute organ deterioration. Controlling this condition usually does not require hospitalization, only oral antihypertensive medication with frequent outpatient monitoring and dose adjustment as needed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hospitalization with intravenous antihypertensives is reserved for hypertensive emergencies, which require evidence of end-organ damage such as heart failure, MI, stroke, or acute kidney injury. This patient lacks such findings.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lifestyle modification alone is appropriate for stage 1 hypertension. A blood pressure of 192/126 mm Hg with severe elevation requires pharmacologic intervention, not lifestyle changes alone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Emergent CT angiography for aortic dissection would be indicated if the patient had symptoms such as tearing chest pain radiating to the back, pulse discrepancies, or a widened mediastinum, none of which are present here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Simple observation without initiating treatment is inappropriate given the severely elevated blood pressure, which carries significant risk for complications such as stroke and myocardial infarction.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with a 20-year history of hypertension is found to have an echocardiogram demonstrating concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with impaired relaxation on Doppler assessment. His ejection fraction is 60%. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of his cardiac dysfunction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased afterload causing ventricular wall thickening with poor diastolic filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Volume overload leading to eccentric ventricular dilation and systolic failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myocardial ischemia causing segmental wall motion abnormalities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Valvular regurgitation resulting in progressive ventricular cavity enlargement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pericardial constriction restricting overall cardiac filling during diastole

explanation.summary: Chronic hypertension increases afterload, forcing the left ventricle to pump against high systemic vascular resistance. The ventricle compensates by thickening (concentric hypertrophy), which impairs relaxation and diastolic filling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Persistent high blood pressure increases cardiac demand as the heart must pump against high SVR. The increased afterload increases cardiac work and left ventricular pressure. The left ventricle thickens (concentric hypertrophy), does not relax well during diastole (diastolic dysfunction), and the reduced LV cavity leads to poor filling and reduced stroke volume, causing nonsystolic (diastolic) heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Eccentric ventricular dilation with systolic failure (dilated cardiomyopathy) results from volume overload or primary myocardial disease, whereas this patient has concentric hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocardial ischemia causes segmental wall motion abnormalities from coronary artery disease, not the global concentric hypertrophy pattern with impaired relaxation described in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Valvular regurgitation causes volume overload with progressive cavity enlargement and eccentric hypertrophy, which is distinct from the pressure-overload concentric hypertrophy seen in chronic hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pericardial constriction restricts filling of both ventricles and presents with signs of right heart failure and equalization of diastolic pressures, which differs from isolated LV diastolic dysfunction.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man presents for a routine physical examination. He has no symptoms. His blood pressure today is 136/84 mm Hg. A repeat reading 3 weeks later is 138/86 mm Hg. His BMI is 31, and he reports consuming fast food regularly and adding salt to meals. Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. Urinalysis and serum creatinine are normal. Which of the following is the most likely type of hypertension in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension secondary to chronic kidney disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension secondary to renal artery stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertension secondary to obstructive sleep apnea

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primary (essential) hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: White coat hypertension with normal ambulatory readings

explanation.summary: Primary hypertension is high blood pressure without an identifiable cause and accounts for approximately 95% of all cases. This patient has modifiable risk factors (obesity, high sodium intake) with no evidence of secondary causes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary hypertension accounts for 95% of hypertension cases and has no identifiable underlying cause. This patient's elevated readings on two occasions at least 2 weeks apart confirm hypertension. His modifiable risk factors (obesity, high sodium diet) are consistent with primary hypertension, and there is no evidence of secondary causes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chronic kidney disease as a cause of secondary hypertension would be expected to show abnormalities on urinalysis (proteinuria, hematuria) or elevated serum creatinine, both of which are normal in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Renal artery stenosis typically presents with an abdominal bruit on physical examination, resistant hypertension, or acute kidney injury after starting ACE inhibitors, none of which are present here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Obstructive sleep apnea as a secondary cause would typically present with daytime somnolence, loud snoring, and witnessed apneas. No such history is mentioned, and secondary causes account for only 5% of cases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: White coat hypertension would show elevated readings only in the clinical setting, with normal ambulatory or home readings. This patient had consistently elevated readings on two separate occasions.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with a 10-year history of poorly controlled hypertension presents for a routine eye examination. Fundoscopic examination reveals copper-wiring of arterioles, arteriovenous nicking, and scattered dot-blot hemorrhages. Which of the following best explains the arteriovenous nicking observed on fundoscopy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Microaneurysm formation at arteriolar-venular crossing points

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thrombotic occlusion of retinal venules caused by elevated venous pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cotton-wool spot formation from ischemic injury to the nerve fiber layer

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neovascularization at the optic disc extending into the vitreous humor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intimal thickening of arterioles compressing adjacent venules at crossing sites

explanation.summary: Arteriovenous nicking in hypertensive retinopathy results from intimal thickening of arterioles, which compresses adjacent venules at crossing points.
explanation.whyCorrect: Persistently elevated blood pressure causes atherosclerotic narrowing of retinal arteries and intimal thickening. Arteriovenous nicking occurs when thickened arterioles compress the underlying venules at crossing sites. This is a classic finding in chronic hypertensive retinopathy along with other vessel changes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Microaneurysms are small outpouchings of vessel walls that can occur in hypertension and diabetes but are not the mechanism underlying arteriovenous nicking, which involves arteriolar compression of venules.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombotic occlusion of retinal venules describes branch retinal vein occlusion, a potential complication of hypertension, but this is not the pathologic basis of arteriovenous nicking itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cotton-wool spots represent areas of ischemic injury to the retinal nerve fiber layer and are a separate finding in hypertensive retinopathy, not the mechanism of arteriovenous nicking.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neovascularization at the optic disc is characteristic of proliferative diabetic retinopathy, not hypertensive retinopathy. Hypertensive retinopathy is characterized by arteriolar changes, hemorrhages, and AV nicking.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe headache, left-sided hemiplegia, and altered consciousness. His blood pressure is 210/130 mm Hg. CT of the head shows a 3-cm hemorrhage in the right putamen. Which of the following is the most likely vascular pathology responsible for this hemorrhagic stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rupture of a saccular aneurysm at the anterior communicating artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rupture of microaneurysms in small penetrating arteries weakened by chronic hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bleeding from a cerebral arteriovenous malformation in the basal ganglia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dissection of a major intracranial artery with intramural hematoma extension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hemorrhagic transformation of a large territorial ischemic infarction

explanation.summary: Chronic uncontrolled hypertension leads to formation of microaneurysms (Charcot-Bouchard aneurysms) in small penetrating arteries, particularly in the basal ganglia. Rupture of these microaneurysms is the most common cause of hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uncontrolled hypertension over time leads to small outpouchings of vessel walls (microaneurysms) in small penetrating arteries. These microaneurysms are prone to rupture, causing hemorrhagic strokes. The putamen/basal ganglia is the most common location for hypertensive hemorrhage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Saccular (berry) aneurysm rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage, not intraparenchymal hemorrhage in the putamen. These aneurysms are located at the circle of Willis, not in small penetrating arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Arteriovenous malformations can cause intracerebral hemorrhage, but they are congenital vascular anomalies that are far less common than hypertensive hemorrhage as a cause of deep basal ganglia bleeds in elderly hypertensive patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intracranial artery dissection typically involves larger vessels and presents with subarachnoid hemorrhage or ischemic stroke, not a discrete deep basal ganglia hemorrhage in the setting of long-standing hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemorrhagic transformation of an ischemic infarct would require evidence of a preceding ischemic event and typically follows a vascular territory distribution rather than presenting as a discrete deep hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents for a follow-up visit. His blood pressure readings over three visits have averaged 142/94 mm Hg. He is asymptomatic. Laboratory studies show a serum creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL, and urinalysis is unremarkable. A chest X-ray and 12-lead ECG are ordered. Which of the following findings on the ECG would most specifically suggest chronic hypertension-related end-organ damage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 105 beats per minute

choices.label: B
choices.text: First-degree atrioventricular block with a PR interval of 220 ms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased QRS voltage with left-axis deviation consistent with left ventricular hypertrophy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diffuse ST-segment elevation with PR depression in multiple leads

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right bundle branch block with right-axis deviation on the frontal plane

explanation.summary: A 12-lead ECG is ordered in hypertensive patients specifically to evaluate for left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH), which is a direct consequence of chronic pressure overload from hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: According to the diagnostic workup for hypertension, a 12-lead ECG is ordered specifically to evaluate for left ventricular hypertrophy. Chronic hypertension increases afterload, causing the left ventricle to thicken. Increased QRS voltage with left-axis deviation is the classic ECG finding of LVH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sinus tachycardia is a nonspecific finding that can result from anxiety, pain, fever, or deconditioning and does not specifically indicate hypertension-related end-organ damage.
whyWrongByChoice.B: First-degree AV block reflects delayed conduction through the AV node and is not a typical consequence of chronic hypertension-related cardiac remodeling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diffuse ST-segment elevation with PR depression suggests acute pericarditis, which is an inflammatory condition of the pericardium unrelated to chronic hypertensive end-organ damage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Right bundle branch block with right-axis deviation suggests right heart pathology, such as pulmonary hypertension or right ventricular strain, rather than the left ventricular changes expected from systemic hypertension.

questions.stem: A 51-year-old man presents for an annual physical examination. His blood pressure today is 148/96 mm Hg, and a repeat reading 4 weeks later is 150/94 mm Hg. He has no family history of endocrine disorders. Physical examination reveals no abdominal bruits or signs of cortisol excess. Which of the following physical examination findings was the clinician most likely attempting to detect by listening for abdominal bruits?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coarctation of the aorta causing turbulent flow in the descending aorta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abdominal aortic aneurysm with associated turbulent flow in the dilated segment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mesenteric ischemia from atherosclerotic disease of the celiac and superior mesenteric arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renal artery stenosis causing turbulent blood flow through narrowed renal arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pheochromocytoma with associated increased blood flow to the adrenal glands

explanation.summary: When evaluating for secondary hypertension, listening for abdominal bruits is performed to detect renal artery stenosis, which is a common secondary cause of hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: The main physical exam approach to evaluate for secondary hypertension includes listening for abdominal bruits, which are sounds heard in renal artery stenosis. Renal artery stenosis is one of the most common causes of secondary hypertension, and turbulent flow through the narrowed renal arteries produces an audible bruit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coarctation of the aorta is typically detected by blood pressure discrepancies between the upper and lower extremities and a systolic murmur heard over the back, not by listening specifically for abdominal bruits.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Abdominal aortic aneurysm may produce a pulsatile abdominal mass and a bruit, but auscultation for bruits in the workup of hypertension is specifically aimed at detecting renal artery stenosis as a secondary cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mesenteric ischemia presents with postprandial abdominal pain and weight loss. While mesenteric bruits may be heard, this is not a recognized secondary cause of hypertension evaluated during the hypertension workup.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pheochromocytoma is an adrenal tumor that causes episodic hypertension through excess catecholamine secretion. It is diagnosed by 24-hour urine metanephrines, not by auscultation for abdominal bruits.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old Black man with a 5-year history of hypertension presents for routine follow-up. His blood pressure is 158/98 mm Hg despite lifestyle modifications. A comprehensive metabolic panel shows a creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL six months ago), and urinalysis shows 2+ protein. Which of the following best explains the renal findings in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension-induced narrowing of small renal arteries impairing filtration function

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immune complex deposition in the glomerular basement membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ascending urinary tract infection causing acute tubular necrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Polycystic kidney disease with progressive cyst enlargement and renal compression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Renal vein thrombosis with secondary glomerular congestion and proteinuria

explanation.summary: Uncontrolled hypertension leads to narrowing of small renal arteries, which worsens the kidney's filtering function and can progress to chronic kidney disease with proteinuria and elevated creatinine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uncontrolled high blood pressure can lead to narrowing of the small renal arteries, which worsens the kidney's filtering function. These alterations decrease blood delivery to the kidneys, and with time those changes can progress to chronic kidney disease, manifesting as rising creatinine and proteinuria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Immune complex deposition causes glomerulonephritis (e.g., lupus nephritis, post-infectious GN) and would present with additional findings such as complement consumption, hematuria with RBC casts, and systemic symptoms not described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ascending urinary tract infections cause pyelonephritis with symptoms such as fever, dysuria, and flank pain, not the insidious decline in renal function seen with chronic hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Polycystic kidney disease is a genetic condition presenting with enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts, family history, and often flank pain, none of which are described in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal vein thrombosis typically presents acutely with flank pain and is associated with nephrotic syndrome or hypercoagulable states, not with the gradual decline in renal function seen in hypertensive nephrosclerosis.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe chest pain, dyspnea, and bilateral crackles on lung auscultation. Her blood pressure is 220/128 mm Hg, oxygen saturation is 88% on room air, and a chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary edema. Troponin is elevated. Which of the following best classifies this patient's blood pressure condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stage 2 hypertension requiring initiation of oral antihypertensives

choices.label: B
choices.text: Severe asymptomatic hypertension manageable in the outpatient setting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Primary hypertension presenting with typical symptomatology

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secondary hypertension caused by an underlying endocrine disorder

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypertensive emergency requiring hospitalization and intravenous antihypertensives

explanation.summary: This patient has severely elevated blood pressure (≥180/120 mm Hg) with evidence of end-organ damage (pulmonary edema indicating heart failure and elevated troponin suggesting MI), defining a hypertensive emergency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypertensive emergency is defined as severely elevated systolic BP (≥180 mm Hg) and/or diastolic BP (≥120 mm Hg) with signs of acute organ injury. This patient has pulmonary edema (heart failure), elevated troponin (myocardial infarction), and requires immediate hospitalization and IV antihypertensives.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stage 2 hypertension is defined as systolic BP ≥140 mm Hg or diastolic ≥90 mm Hg. While this patient's BP exceeds these thresholds, the presence of end-organ damage elevates this to a hypertensive emergency requiring IV treatment, not merely oral agents.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Severe asymptomatic hypertension (formerly hypertensive urgency) has BP ≥180/120 mm Hg but no signs of end-organ damage. This patient has clear evidence of organ injury (pulmonary edema, elevated troponin), ruling out this classification.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Primary hypertension is typically asymptomatic and discovered incidentally. The acute presentation with chest pain, dyspnea, and organ damage represents a hypertensive emergency, not the usual presentation of primary hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While secondary hypertension from an endocrine disorder is possible, there is no clinical evidence suggesting an endocrine etiology. The immediate priority is treating the hypertensive emergency, not pursuing a secondary cause.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has blood pressure readings of 124/82 mm Hg and 126/78 mm Hg on two separate office visits 3 weeks apart. She has no comorbidities. According to current classification guidelines, which of the following best categorizes this patient's blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal blood pressure requiring no further intervention

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated blood pressure warranting lifestyle modifications

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stage 1 hypertension requiring lifestyle modifications and possible medication

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stage 2 hypertension requiring initiation of pharmacologic therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Severe asymptomatic hypertension requiring close outpatient follow-up

explanation.summary: Systolic blood pressure of 120-129 mm Hg and/or diastolic 80-89 mm Hg is classified as elevated blood pressure, which warrants lifestyle modifications.
explanation.whyCorrect: According to the blood pressure classification table, elevated blood pressure is defined as systolic 120-129 mm Hg and/or diastolic 80-89 mm Hg. This patient's average readings of approximately 125/80 mm Hg fall into this category. Lifestyle modifications are the appropriate intervention at this stage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Normal blood pressure is defined as systolic <120 mm Hg and diastolic <80 mm Hg. This patient's systolic readings of 124-126 mm Hg exceed the normal threshold, placing her in the elevated category.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stage 1 hypertension is defined as systolic 130-139 mm Hg and/or diastolic 80-89 mm Hg. This patient's systolic readings are consistently below 130 mm Hg, so she does not meet criteria for stage 1 hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stage 2 hypertension requires systolic BP ≥140 mm Hg and/or diastolic ≥90 mm Hg. This patient's readings are well below these thresholds.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Severe asymptomatic hypertension requires systolic BP ≥180 mm Hg and/or diastolic ≥120 mm Hg. This patient's blood pressure is significantly below these values.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of chronic hypertension undergoes a renal biopsy for evaluation of progressive proteinuria. Histologic examination of a renal arteriole shows a markedly thickened wall with homogeneous, eosinophilic material deposited within the vessel wall and a significantly narrowed lumen. Which of the following best describes this vascular pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis with concentric smooth muscle cell proliferation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis with inflammatory cell infiltration of the vessel wall

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque formation with lipid core and fibrous cap development

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hyaline arteriolosclerosis with protein deposition and luminal narrowing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Medial calcific sclerosis affecting the tunica media without luminal compromise

explanation.summary: Hyaline arteriolosclerosis is characterized by homogeneous, eosinophilic protein deposition (hyalinization) in arteriolar walls with luminal narrowing, and is a hallmark finding of chronic hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: The described histologic finding of a markedly thickened arteriole with protein deposition (hyalinization) and markedly narrowed lumen is classic hyaline arteriolosclerosis, which is a characteristic vascular pathology of chronic hypertension as described in the source material.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis (onion-skinning) features concentric laminated smooth muscle cell proliferation causing luminal obliteration, which is associated with malignant hypertension rather than the homogeneous eosinophilic material described here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrinoid necrosis involves acute destruction of the vessel wall with fibrin deposition and inflammatory infiltrate, seen in malignant hypertension or vasculitis, not the chronic protein deposition described in this case.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atherosclerotic plaques with lipid cores and fibrous caps occur in large and medium-sized arteries, not in arterioles. The described pathology specifically involves an arteriole with hyaline change.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Medial calcific sclerosis (Monckeberg sclerosis) affects the tunica media of medium-sized muscular arteries with calcium deposition but does not narrow the lumen, unlike the luminal narrowing described here.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed hypertension is started on lisinopril. At a 4-week follow-up, he reports a persistent dry cough that began shortly after starting the medication. His blood pressure is well controlled at 128/78 mm Hg. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this adverse effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bronchospasm from non-selective beta-receptor blockade in the airways

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reflex stimulation of cough receptors from rapid blood pressure reduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Accumulation of bradykinin due to inhibition of the angiotensin-converting enzyme

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct irritation of the pharyngeal mucosa by the active metabolite of the drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased angiotensin II levels causing airway smooth muscle constriction

explanation.summary: ACE inhibitors cause dry cough through accumulation of bradykinin, which is normally degraded by ACE. When ACE is inhibited, bradykinin accumulates and stimulates cough receptors in the airway.
explanation.whyCorrect: A dry cough is a well-known adverse effect of ACE inhibitors caused by the accumulation of bradykinin. ACE normally degrades bradykinin, so inhibition of ACE leads to bradykinin accumulation, which stimulates cough receptors. Decreasing the dose or switching to a different medication class is effective to stop the dry cough.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bronchospasm from non-selective beta-receptor blockade is an adverse effect of non-selective beta-blockers (e.g., propranolol), not ACE inhibitors. Lisinopril does not block beta-receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Reflex cough from rapid blood pressure reduction is not a recognized mechanism for ACE inhibitor-induced cough. The cough is specifically due to bradykinin accumulation, not hemodynamic changes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct mucosal irritation is not the mechanism of ACE inhibitor cough. The effect is mediated through bradykinin, a vasoactive peptide, not through direct tissue irritation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ACE inhibitors decrease angiotensin II levels by preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II. They do not increase angiotensin II levels; this is the opposite of their mechanism of action.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with stage 2 hypertension and type 2 diabetes with proteinuria is being evaluated for antihypertensive therapy. Her blood pressure is 156/98 mm Hg. Which of the following classes of antihypertensive medications provides the greatest renal protective benefit in this patient due to its ability to dilate the efferent arteriole?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thiazide diuretics such as hydrochlorothiazide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers such as amlodipine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alpha-1 adrenergic blockers such as prazosin

choices.label: D
choices.text: ACE inhibitors such as ramipril

choices.label: E
choices.text: Centrally acting alpha-2 agonists such as clonidine

explanation.summary: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred in hypertensive patients with proteinuria due to their ability to dilate the efferent arteriole, reducing intraglomerular pressure and providing renal protection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Given their ability to dilate the efferent arteriole, ACE inhibitors and ARBs are often used to treat hypertension in patients with proteinuria. By reducing intraglomerular pressure, they slow the progression of kidney disease, making them the preferred choice in this diabetic patient with proteinuria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thiazide diuretics lower blood pressure through volume reduction by inhibiting the Na-Cl symporter in the distal convoluted tubule. They do not specifically dilate the efferent arteriole or provide the same degree of renal protection in proteinuric patients.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dihydropyridine CCBs decrease SVR through arteriolar vasodilation but primarily dilate the afferent arteriole rather than the efferent arteriole, which may actually increase intraglomerular pressure and worsen proteinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha-1 blockers decrease peripheral vascular resistance by relaxing arterial and venous smooth muscle but are no longer first-line agents for hypertension and do not specifically target the efferent arteriole.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clonidine reduces sympathetic outflow centrally and is considered a last-line agent. It does not specifically dilate the efferent arteriole or provide the targeted renal protection seen with ACE inhibitors.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with primary hypertension undergoes evaluation of the pathophysiologic mechanisms contributing to his elevated blood pressure. Plasma renin activity is elevated. Which of the following best describes the pathway by which renin contributes to hypertension in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Renin directly constricts arteriolar smooth muscle to increase systemic vascular resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Renin increases angiotensin II and aldosterone, causing vasoconstriction and sodium-water retention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renin activates the complement cascade, promoting vascular inflammation and vasoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renin stimulates catecholamine release from the adrenal cortex to raise cardiac output

choices.label: E
choices.text: Renin inhibits prostaglandin synthesis, preventing vasodilation of renal afferent arterioles

explanation.summary: Renin activates the RAAS cascade: renin increases angiotensin II (which causes vasoconstriction and increases SVR) and aldosterone (which increases renal sodium and water retention, expanding blood volume).
explanation.whyCorrect: The sympathetic nervous system causes the kidney to release renin, which increases angiotensin II (AT-II) and aldosterone levels. AT-II increases SVR through vasoconstriction of vascular smooth muscle. Aldosterone increases renal retention of sodium and water, leading to increased blood volume, increased venous return, and higher stroke volume, all of which elevate blood pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Renin does not directly constrict arteriolar smooth muscle. It is an enzyme that cleaves angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, which is then converted to angiotensin II by ACE. It is angiotensin II that causes vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Renin does not activate the complement cascade. The RAAS and complement systems are separate pathways. Renin specifically initiates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Catecholamines are released from the adrenal medulla (not cortex) by sympathetic stimulation, not by renin. Renin acts through the RAAS pathway, which involves aldosterone from the adrenal cortex, not catecholamines.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renin does not inhibit prostaglandin synthesis. Prostaglandin synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of NSAIDs, not renin. Renin acts through the angiotensin-aldosterone pathway.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman with stage 1 hypertension is started on amlodipine. Two weeks later, she presents with bilateral ankle swelling. Her blood pressure is 132/80 mm Hg. Cardiac examination is normal, and there is no jugular venous distension. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of her peripheral edema?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased cardiac output leading to systemic venous congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypoalbuminemia from drug-induced hepatotoxicity reducing oncotic pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sodium and water retention from inhibition of the distal tubule Na-Cl cotransporter

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allergic reaction causing increased vascular permeability and tissue swelling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure from arteriolar vasodilation

explanation.summary: Dihydropyridine CCBs like amlodipine cause peripheral edema through fluid extravasation secondary to increased capillary hydrostatic pressure in dilated vessels, not from heart failure or fluid retention.
explanation.whyCorrect: A well-known adverse effect of CCBs is peripheral edema from fluid extravasation secondary to increased capillary hydrostatic pressure in dilated vessels. The arteriolar vasodilation caused by CCBs increases pressure in the downstream capillary bed, driving fluid into the interstitial space.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased cardiac output with systemic venous congestion would suggest heart failure, which would present with jugular venous distension, S3, and other signs. This patient has a normal cardiac exam, indicating the edema is not from cardiac dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug-induced hepatotoxicity with hypoalbuminemia is not a recognized adverse effect of amlodipine. CCB-induced edema results from hemodynamic changes in capillary pressure, not from reduced oncotic pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sodium and water retention from Na-Cl cotransporter inhibition describes the mechanism of thiazide diuretics, not calcium channel blockers. CCBs act by inhibiting calcium entry into smooth muscle cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An allergic reaction causing increased vascular permeability would typically present with urticaria, pruritus, and potentially angioedema, not isolated bilateral ankle swelling. The bilateral, dependent edema pattern is consistent with a hemodynamic effect.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with resistant hypertension is taking lisinopril, amlodipine, and hydrochlorothiazide at optimal doses with confirmed adherence. His blood pressure remains 152/96 mm Hg on three consecutive visits. Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring confirms persistent elevation. According to current guidelines, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Screen for secondary causes of hypertension such as primary aldosteronism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add a fourth antihypertensive agent from a different drug class

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discontinue all medications and pursue aggressive lifestyle modifications

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increase lisinopril to maximum dose before considering additional interventions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Refer for renal denervation as definitive treatment for resistant hypertension

explanation.summary: In confirmed resistant hypertension (failure to achieve targets despite multiple medications at optimal doses with confirmed adherence), guidelines recommend screening for secondary causes such as primary aldosteronism and renal artery stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: For patients who fail to achieve blood pressure targets despite multiple medications at optimal doses (resistant hypertension), the guidelines recommend confirming true resistance by ruling out white-coat hypertension and nonadherence (both done here), then screening for secondary causes of hypertension such as primary aldosteronism and renal artery stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Adding a fourth agent without first screening for secondary causes may be premature. The systematic approach for resistant hypertension prioritizes identifying and addressing reversible secondary causes before adding more medications.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Discontinuing all antihypertensive medications in a patient with resistant hypertension and multiple cardiovascular risk factors could be dangerous and is not recommended. Lifestyle modifications should be concurrent with pharmacotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The question states medications are already at optimal doses. Increasing lisinopril dose would not be appropriate if it is already optimized, and the priority is to investigate secondary causes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal denervation is an investigational procedure and not established as a standard recommendation in current guidelines for resistant hypertension. The systematic approach should first evaluate for treatable secondary causes.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with primary hypertension has been eating a high-sodium diet for years. Despite normal renal function, his blood pressure increases significantly whenever he consumes excess salt, more so than the average hypertensive patient. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms best explains this patient's exaggerated blood pressure response to sodium intake?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Impaired baroreceptor reflex leading to excessive sympathetic activation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased renal renin secretion in response to high sodium concentration in the macula densa

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exaggerated sodium retention expanding extracellular fluid volume and increasing stroke volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct toxic effect of sodium on arteriolar smooth muscle causing irreversible vasoconstriction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sodium-induced suppression of aldosterone with paradoxical fluid retention in the collecting duct

explanation.summary: Salt-sensitive patients experience an exaggerated increase in blood pressure with sodium intake because sodium retention expands extracellular fluid and blood volume, increasing stroke volume and cardiac output, which drives up blood pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: In salt-sensitive patients, increased salt intake leads to an exaggerated increase in blood pressure. Sodium retention expands extracellular fluid and blood volume, increasing stroke volume. In response to increased cardiac output, peripheral vessels constrict, raising SVR and contributing to elevated blood pressure. About 50% of hypertensive patients may be salt-sensitive.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While baroreceptor dysfunction can contribute to hypertension, the exaggerated response to sodium specifically involves volume expansion and increased cardiac output, not primarily a baroreceptor reflex abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.B: High sodium delivery to the macula densa actually suppresses renin secretion through tubuloglomerular feedback, not increases it. This is the opposite of the expected physiologic response.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sodium does not have a direct toxic effect causing irreversible vasoconstriction of arteriolar smooth muscle. The salt-sensitive mechanism involves volume expansion and subsequent peripheral vasoconstriction as a compensatory response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High sodium intake suppresses aldosterone as part of normal RAAS regulation, but salt-sensitive hypertension is driven by sodium retention expanding volume, not by paradoxical aldosterone-independent fluid retention in the collecting duct.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension is started on prazosin. Two days after initiating treatment, she stands up from a seated position and experiences severe lightheadedness, near-syncope, and a fall. Her blood pressure is 88/54 mm Hg while standing and 126/80 mm Hg while supine. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this adverse reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excessive beta-1 receptor blockade reducing cardiac output and heart rate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Central alpha-2 agonism causing excessive suppression of sympathetic outflow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of calcium entry into vascular smooth muscle causing peripheral vasodilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Competitive blockade of alpha-1 adrenoceptors causing relaxation of arterial and venous smooth muscle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Potassium channel opening in vascular smooth muscle causing membrane hyperpolarization

explanation.summary: Prazosin is an alpha-1 adrenergic blocker that causes orthostatic hypotension by competitively blocking alpha-1 receptors, leading to relaxation of arterial and venous smooth muscle and decreased peripheral vascular resistance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alpha-1 blockers like prazosin produce competitive blockade of alpha-1 adrenoceptors, decreasing vasoconstriction and causing relaxation of both arterial and venous smooth muscle. This decreases peripheral vascular resistance and lowers blood pressure. Orthostatic hypotension is a significant adverse effect, with higher risk at the first dose and with rapid dose increases. Prazosin has a higher risk due to its rapid onset of action.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-1 receptor blockade reducing cardiac output and heart rate describes the mechanism of beta-blockers (e.g., metoprolol), not alpha-1 blockers like prazosin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Central alpha-2 agonism describes the mechanism of clonidine, which inhibits sympathetic vasomotor centers centrally. Prazosin acts peripherally at alpha-1 receptors, not centrally at alpha-2 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibition of calcium entry into vascular smooth muscle describes calcium channel blockers (e.g., amlodipine, nifedipine), not alpha-1 adrenergic blockers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Potassium channel opening causing membrane hyperpolarization describes the mechanism of minoxidil, not prazosin. Minoxidil's active metabolite opens potassium channels to decrease vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with stage 2 hypertension (BP 154/96 mm Hg on two separate visits). She has no other comorbidities. According to current guidelines, which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monotherapy with a beta-blocker such as metoprolol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Combination of an alpha-1 blocker and a centrally acting alpha-2 agonist

choices.label: C
choices.text: Monotherapy with a single first-line agent with reassessment in 6 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Triple therapy with three first-line agents to achieve rapid blood pressure control

choices.label: E
choices.text: A single-pill fixed-dose combination of two first-line agents from different classes

explanation.summary: For stage 2 hypertension, guidelines recommend initiating two first-line agents from different classes, with a single-pill fixed-dose combination preferred over two separate pills to improve adherence.
explanation.whyCorrect: In stage 2 hypertension, two first-line agents from different classes can be chosen. A single pill, fixed-dose combination is preferred over two different pills, as this has been shown to improve adherence. First-line agents include thiazide-type diuretics, CCBs, ACEi, or ARBs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-blockers are not recommended as first-line agents for hypertension unless the patient has comorbidities such as coronary heart disease or heart failure. Additionally, monotherapy is insufficient for stage 2 hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-1 blockers are no longer first-line agents, and alpha-2 agonists like clonidine are considered last-line agents. Neither class is appropriate for initial therapy of stage 2 hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Single first-line agent monotherapy is recommended for stage 1 hypertension, not stage 2 hypertension, which typically requires initiation of two agents from different classes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Triple therapy with three agents is not the standard initial approach for stage 2 hypertension. Guidelines recommend starting with two agents and adjusting therapy based on response before adding additional medications.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with recently diagnosed primary hypertension asks about the cause of his condition. He has a strong family history of hypertension, but no identifiable secondary cause has been found. Which of the following statements best characterizes the role of genetics in primary hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single autosomal dominant gene mutation has been identified as the primary cause

choices.label: B
choices.text: Genetic factors play no significant role; environmental factors are solely responsible

choices.label: C
choices.text: Primary hypertension is heritable with a significant genetic component, though no single gene has been primarily linked

choices.label: D
choices.text: Genetic testing can reliably predict the development of primary hypertension in at-risk individuals

choices.label: E
choices.text: The genetic component of primary hypertension is limited exclusively to salt sensitivity traits

explanation.summary: Although no specific gene has been primarily linked to primary hypertension, a significant genetic component is likely, as primary hypertension is heritable.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that although no specific gene has been primarily linked to primary hypertension, a significant genetic component is likely, as primary hypertension is heritable. This reflects the polygenic and multifactorial nature of the condition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Primary hypertension is not caused by a single autosomal dominant gene. It is a complex clinical syndrome with likely polygenic inheritance and multiple contributing environmental factors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Genetic factors do play a significant role, as primary hypertension is heritable. Both modifiable (environmental) and nonmodifiable (genetic) risk factors contribute to the development of hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No reliable genetic test exists to predict primary hypertension because no single causative gene has been identified. The condition results from complex interactions between multiple genetic and environmental factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While salt sensitivity may have a genetic component, the heritability of primary hypertension extends beyond salt sensitivity alone. Multiple pathophysiologic mechanisms including SVR, catecholamine sensitivity, and RAAS activity are involved.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with primary hypertension and coronary artery disease is being evaluated for antihypertensive therapy adjustment. His current blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg. He also has a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Which of the following antihypertensive agents, while not a first-line drug for hypertension, would be most appropriate to add to his regimen given his comorbidities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nifedipine, a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metoprolol, a beta-1 selective adrenergic antagonist

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prazosin, an alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clonidine, a centrally acting alpha-2 adrenergic agonist

choices.label: E
choices.text: Minoxidil, a potassium channel opener with direct vasodilatory action

explanation.summary: Although beta-blockers are not recommended as first-line agents for hypertension, they are specifically indicated in patients with coronary heart disease or heart failure, making metoprolol the most appropriate choice for this patient.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-blockers are not recommended for first-line use for hypertension; however, they could be used to manage hypertension in patients with coronary heart disease or heart failure. Metoprolol reduces heart rate and contractility, decreasing cardiac output and myocardial oxygen demand, which benefits both conditions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dihydropyridine CCBs like nifedipine can cause reflex tachycardia, making them risky in most forms of ischemic heart disease. This patient has coronary artery disease, making nifedipine a potentially harmful choice.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prazosin is an alpha-1 blocker that is no longer used as a first-line agent. It carries significant risk of orthostatic hypotension and does not provide the specific cardiac benefits needed in coronary artery disease and heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Clonidine is considered a last-line agent for hypertension that has not responded to two or more drugs. Its side effects (sedation, dry mouth, constipation) and lack of specific cardiac benefit make it less appropriate than metoprolol.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Minoxidil can cause reflex tachycardia through baroreceptor-mediated sympathetic activation, which would be detrimental in a patient with coronary artery disease and heart failure by increasing myocardial oxygen demand.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with hypertension has been taking hydrochlorothiazide for 6 months. She presents with muscle cramps and fatigue. Laboratory studies reveal serum sodium of 131 mEq/L, serum potassium of 3.0 mEq/L, and serum uric acid of 9.2 mg/dL. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which hydrochlorothiazide causes these electrolyte abnormalities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of the Na-Cl symporter in the distal convoluted tubule increasing excretion of sodium, potassium, and chloride

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blockade of the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of the epithelial sodium channel in the collecting duct causing potassium wasting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct inhibition of aldosterone receptors in the collecting duct causing sodium loss

choices.label: E
choices.text: Competitive inhibition of carbonic anhydrase in the proximal convoluted tubule

explanation.summary: Thiazide diuretics inhibit the Na-Cl symporter in the distal convoluted tubule, resulting in increased excretion of sodium, potassium, and chloride, leading to hyponatremia and hypokalemia. Hyperuricemia is also a recognized adverse effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thiazides inhibit the Na-Cl symporter in the distal convoluted tubule, resulting in increased excretion of Na, K, Cl, and water by the kidneys. This mechanism explains this patient's hyponatremia and hypokalemia. Hyperuricemia is also a recognized adverse effect of thiazide-type diuretics.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Blockade of the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the thick ascending limb describes loop diuretics like furosemide, not thiazide diuretics. Thiazides act more distally in the nephron at the distal convoluted tubule.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibition of the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the collecting duct describes the mechanism of potassium-sparing diuretics like amiloride, not thiazide diuretics, which act on the distal convoluted tubule Na-Cl symporter.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct inhibition of aldosterone receptors describes mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists like spironolactone, which actually cause potassium retention rather than potassium wasting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Competitive inhibition of carbonic anhydrase describes acetazolamide, which acts in the proximal tubule. Thiazides do not primarily inhibit carbonic anhydrase and act at a different nephron segment.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old woman with primary hypertension has an isolated systolic blood pressure of 168 mm Hg with a diastolic pressure of 72 mm Hg. She has no other cardiovascular comorbidities. Which of the following age-related vascular changes most directly contributes to her isolated systolic hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased venous capacitance reducing effective circulating volume

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enhanced endothelial nitric oxide production causing arteriolar dilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased parasympathetic tone causing reduced heart rate and stroke volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: Age-related decline in aldosterone secretion reducing sodium reabsorption

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased vascular compliance from arterial hardening and thickening with age

explanation.summary: With increasing age, small arteries harden and thicken, becoming less compliant. For a given volume of blood, the less compliant vessels generate more resistance, and the loss of compliance especially elevates systolic blood pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: With increasing age, small arteries harden and thicken, ultimately becoming less compliant. For a given volume of blood entering the arteriole, the vessels can expand less, generate more resistance to flow, and pressures increase. The loss of compliance increases SVR and especially elevates the systolic blood pressure, explaining this patient's isolated systolic hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased venous capacitance would reduce preload and cardiac output, potentially lowering blood pressure rather than increasing it. Age-related arterial stiffening, not venous changes, drives isolated systolic hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Enhanced endothelial nitric oxide production would cause vasodilation and lower blood pressure. In aging, nitric oxide bioavailability actually decreases, contributing to increased vascular stiffness and higher blood pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased parasympathetic tone would reduce heart rate and is not a characteristic age-related change that contributes to isolated systolic hypertension. The primary mechanism is decreased arterial compliance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Age-related decline in aldosterone secretion would reduce sodium and water reabsorption, potentially lowering blood volume and blood pressure. The primary mechanism of isolated systolic hypertension in the elderly is arterial stiffening, not aldosterone changes.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician for evaluation of newly discovered hypertension. She has no significant past medical history, does not smoke, and has a BMI of 23. Her blood pressure is 158/98 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals an abdominal bruit. Laboratory studies show a serum potassium of 3.2 mEq/L and a mildly elevated serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL. Renal angiography demonstrates a characteristic "string of beads" appearance of the right renal artery. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathology causing this patient's hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque narrowing the renal artery lumen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Overgrowth of arterial smooth muscle causing luminal stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immune complex deposition within the glomerular basement membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aldosterone-producing adenoma of the adrenal cortex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla

explanation.summary: This young woman without atherosclerotic risk factors has renal artery stenosis caused by fibromuscular dysplasia, which is characterized by overgrowth of arterial smooth muscle producing luminal stenosis and a "string of beads" appearance on angiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fibromuscular dysplasia is the most common cause of renal artery stenosis in young females without atherosclerotic risk factors. It is characterized by overgrowth of arterial smooth muscle that causes luminal stenosis. The reduced renal blood flow activates the RAAS, causing hypertension and hypokalemia from aldosterone-mediated potassium loss.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis is seen in older patients with risk factors such as diabetes, hyperlipidemia, or smoking. This young, healthy woman is far more likely to have fibromuscular dysplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Immune complex deposition describes glomerulonephritis, which would present with hematuria and proteinuria rather than a "string of beads" on angiography and an abdominal bruit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn syndrome) could explain hypokalemia with hypertension, but it would not cause an abdominal bruit or the angiographic findings described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A pheochromocytoma would present with episodic symptoms such as headaches, sweating, tremors, and tachycardia, and would not produce an abdominal bruit or angiographic changes in the renal artery.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and peripheral artery disease presents with blood pressure of 172/104 mm Hg despite compliance with three antihypertensive medications at maximum doses. His serum potassium is 3.4 mEq/L and creatinine is 1.6 mg/dL. Doppler ultrasound of the renal arteries reveals a single small right kidney with diminished blood flow. His physician considers adding an ACE inhibitor but is concerned about a potential complication. Which of the following best explains this concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ACE inhibitors may cause reflex tachycardia and worsen coronary ischemia in diabetic patients

choices.label: B
choices.text: ACE inhibitors may cause severe hyperkalemia by directly stimulating aldosterone secretion

choices.label: C
choices.text: ACE inhibitors preferentially dilate the efferent arteriole, potentially causing a dangerous drop in GFR if bilateral stenosis is present

choices.label: D
choices.text: ACE inhibitors may trigger a hypertensive crisis by blocking the compensatory effects of the renin-angiotensin system

choices.label: E
choices.text: ACE inhibitors may accelerate atherosclerotic plaque progression by increasing oxidative stress in renal vessels

explanation.summary: In patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis, ACE inhibitors preferentially dilate the efferent arteriole. Since the kidneys already have reduced perfusion, this can cause a precipitous drop in glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure and GFR, leading to acute kidney injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: ACE inhibitors and ARBs dilate the efferent arteriole by blocking angiotensin II. In bilateral RAS, where renal perfusion is already compromised, this reduces the glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure needed for filtration, potentially causing prerenal acute kidney injury.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACE inhibitors do not cause reflex tachycardia. They lower blood pressure by reducing angiotensin II levels and are generally well-tolerated in diabetic patients.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ACE inhibitors decrease aldosterone secretion (not increase it) by reducing angiotensin II, which normally stimulates aldosterone release. They can cause hyperkalemia by reducing aldosterone-mediated potassium excretion, but the mechanism described is incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ACE inhibitors lower blood pressure by blocking RAAS; they do not trigger hypertensive crises. The concern in bilateral RAS is kidney injury, not paradoxical hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no established mechanism by which ACE inhibitors accelerate atherosclerotic plaque progression. In fact, ACE inhibitors are generally considered cardioprotective.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman presents with fatigue, weight gain, proximal muscle weakness, and blood pressure of 162/100 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals moon facies, truncal obesity, and purple abdominal striae. Laboratory studies show a serum potassium of 3.8 mEq/L, glucose of 180 mg/dL, and an elevated 24-hour urine cortisol. Which of the following mechanisms most likely contributes to this patient's hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased sensitivity of beta-adrenergic receptors to circulating catecholamines

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sympathetic nervous system activation due to intermittent hypoxia during sleep

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased renal blood flow leading to activation of the juxtaglomerular apparatus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased sodium and water retention combined with upregulation of angiotensin-II receptors and reduced nitric oxide release

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hepatic production of angiotensinogen stimulated by exogenous estrogen supplementation

explanation.summary: This patient has Cushing syndrome (hypercortisolism). High cortisol causes hypertension through multiple mechanisms: aldosterone-like effects increasing sodium and water retention, increased expression of angiotensin-II receptors, potentiated catecholamine effects, and reduced nitric oxide release.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypercortisolism causes hypertension via increased sodium and water retention (aldosterone-like effects), increased expression of angiotensin-II receptors, increased effects of catecholamines on adrenergic receptors, and reduced nitric oxide (a vasodilator) release, all of which increase SVR and blood volume.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased beta-receptor sensitivity to catecholamines is the mechanism of hypertension in hyperthyroidism, not hypercortisolism. Cortisol does potentiate catecholamine effects, but through a different mechanism than direct beta-receptor sensitization.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sympathetic activation due to intermittent hypoxia is the mechanism of hypertension in obstructive sleep apnea, not Cushing syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreased renal blood flow activating the juxtaglomerular apparatus describes the mechanism in renal artery stenosis, not hypercortisolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hepatic angiotensinogen production stimulated by estrogen is the mechanism by which oral contraceptive pills cause secondary hypertension, not hypercortisolism.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with episodic headaches, profuse sweating, tremors, and palpitations. Her seated blood pressure is 190/110 mm Hg, and her heart rate is 112 beats/min. When she stands, her blood pressure drops to 148/86 mm Hg. CT of the abdomen reveals a 4-cm left adrenal mass. Which of the following laboratory findings would most strongly support the suspected diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated serum aldosterone with suppressed plasma renin activity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated 24-hour urine cortisol with loss of diurnal cortisol variation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated plasma or 24-hour urine metanephrines

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevated serum thyroid-stimulating hormone with low free T4

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated serum blood urea nitrogen and creatinine with proteinuria

explanation.summary: This patient's episodic sympathetic symptoms (headaches, sweating, tremors, tachycardia), hypertension with orthostatic hypotension, and adrenal mass are classic for pheochromocytoma. Elevated plasma or 24-hour urine metanephrines confirm the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting tumor that presents with episodic symptoms of sympathetic overdrive and orthostatic hypotension despite basal hypertension. Measurement of plasma or 24-hour urine metanephrines is the diagnostic test of choice.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Elevated aldosterone with suppressed renin suggests primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome), which presents with hypertension and hypokalemia but not with episodic sympathetic symptoms or orthostatic hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Elevated 24-hour urine cortisol suggests Cushing syndrome, which presents with truncal obesity, moon facies, and striae rather than episodic sympathetic symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Elevated TSH with low free T4 indicates hypothyroidism, which may cause diastolic hypertension but not episodic sympathetic overdrive symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated BUN, creatinine, and proteinuria suggest chronic kidney disease, which causes hypertension through sodium retention and RAAS activation, not through episodic catecholamine excess.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old obese man presents to the clinic accompanied by his wife, who reports that he snores loudly, frequently stops breathing during sleep, and is excessively sleepy during the day. His blood pressure is 152/96 mm Hg. Serum electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, and urinalysis are all within normal limits. A polysomnography study confirms the suspected diagnosis. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this patient's hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Airway collapse during sleep activates the sympathetic nervous system, increasing cardiac output and peripheral resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic hypoxia causes decreased tubular sodium reabsorption and expanded extracellular fluid volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intermittent airway obstruction triggers excessive aldosterone release from the adrenal cortex

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recurrent apneic episodes cause direct endothelial injury and activation of the coagulation cascade

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pharyngeal muscle collapse increases intrathoracic pressure, directly compressing the aorta and raising systemic blood pressure

explanation.summary: In obstructive sleep apnea, pharyngeal muscles and excess connective tissue collapse during sleep, compressing the airway and momentarily stopping breathing. This activates the sympathetic nervous system, increasing cardiac output and peripheral resistance, leading to hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: OSA causes intermittent airway collapse during sleep, which activates the sympathetic nervous system. The increased sympathetic tone raises both cardiac output and peripheral vascular resistance, resulting in systemic hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.B: OSA does not cause hypertension through decreased tubular sodium reabsorption. That mechanism is associated with chronic kidney disease. The primary mechanism in OSA is sympathetic activation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OSA does not directly trigger excessive aldosterone release. The hypertension in OSA is mediated by sympathetic nervous system activation, not through the RAAS pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct endothelial injury and coagulation cascade activation are features of hypertensive emergency pathophysiology, not the mechanism by which OSA causes chronic hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While intrathoracic pressure changes occur in OSA, the hypertension is not caused by mechanical compression of the aorta. The mechanism is sympathetic activation due to hypoxia from airway obstruction.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with blood pressure of 144/88 mm Hg discovered during a routine physical examination. She has no family history of hypertension. Her BMI is 21. Review of medications reveals daily use of a combined oral contraceptive pill, ibuprofen for chronic knee pain, and pseudoephedrine for nasal congestion. All laboratory tests are normal. Which of the following is the most likely primary mechanism by which her oral contraceptive contributes to her elevated blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alpha-receptor mediated vasoconstriction of peripheral arterioles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct sympathetic nervous system activation leading to increased cardiac output

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibition of cyclooxygenase-2 in the kidneys reducing vasodilating prostaglandins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Estrogen-induced hepatic production of angiotensinogen leading to increased angiotensin-II

choices.label: E
choices.text: Potentiation of catecholamine effects on adrenergic receptors by increasing receptor expression

explanation.summary: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptive pills cause hypertension by inducing hepatic production of angiotensinogen, which is converted to angiotensin-II. Angiotensin-II causes direct vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated sodium and water retention.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oral contraceptive pills containing estrogen increase blood pressure because estrogen induces the liver to produce more angiotensinogen. This substrate is converted to angiotensin-II, which directly causes vasoconstriction and indirectly promotes sodium and water retention through aldosterone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-receptor mediated vasoconstriction is the mechanism by which pseudoephedrine, cocaine, and amphetamines cause hypertension, not oral contraceptives.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Direct sympathetic nervous system activation is the mechanism of hypertension in obstructive sleep apnea and stimulant drugs, not oral contraceptives.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibition of COX-2 in the kidneys is the mechanism by which NSAIDs like ibuprofen cause hypertension, not oral contraceptives.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Potentiation of catecholamine effects through increased receptor expression is a mechanism of cortisol-mediated hypertension in Cushing syndrome, not oral contraceptives.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a 20-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with blood pressure of 168/102 mm Hg. Laboratory studies reveal serum creatinine of 2.8 mg/dL, BUN of 42 mg/dL, proteinuria on urinalysis, and an estimated GFR of 28 mL/min/1.73 m². His physician initiates an antihypertensive medication that is preferred in this clinical setting because it slows disease progression. Which of the following medications was most likely prescribed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amlodipine, a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hydrochlorothiazide, a thiazide diuretic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metoprolol, a selective beta-1 adrenergic antagonist

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clonidine, a centrally-acting alpha-2 agonist

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lisinopril, an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor

explanation.summary: This patient has hypertension secondary to chronic kidney disease. ACE inhibitors (or ARBs) are the preferred antihypertensive agents in CKD because they slow the progression of kidney disease in addition to controlling blood pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are the recommended first-line agents for hypertension in CKD because they reduce intraglomerular pressure by dilating the efferent arteriole, thereby slowing disease progression in addition to lowering systemic blood pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amlodipine can lower blood pressure effectively but does not provide the same renal-protective benefits as ACE inhibitors in CKD. It does not slow CKD progression.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hydrochlorothiazide is a commonly used antihypertensive but does not slow CKD progression. Additionally, thiazide diuretics become less effective at lower GFR values.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Metoprolol can lower blood pressure but does not have the specific renal-protective benefit of ACE inhibitors in slowing CKD progression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Clonidine is not first-line therapy for hypertension in CKD. It does not slow disease progression and has side effects such as sedation and rebound hypertension.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with new-onset hypertension (blood pressure 156/98 mm Hg) and unexplained hypokalemia (serum potassium 2.9 mEq/L). She is not taking any diuretics. CT of the abdomen reveals a 1.5-cm left adrenal mass. Further testing confirms elevated serum aldosterone with suppressed plasma renin activity. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which her condition causes hypokalemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess aldosterone stimulates potassium excretion in the renal collecting ducts

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess aldosterone inhibits the sodium-potassium ATPase in the proximal tubule

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excess aldosterone increases glomerular filtration rate, causing obligatory potassium loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excess aldosterone promotes potassium shift into cells via beta-2 adrenergic receptor activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Excess aldosterone impairs intestinal potassium absorption by downregulating mucosal transporters

explanation.summary: This patient has primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome). Aldosterone acts on the renal collecting ducts to promote sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion, leading to hypokalemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aldosterone acts on principal cells of the renal collecting duct to stimulate sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion. Excess aldosterone causes excessive urinary potassium loss, resulting in hypokalemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aldosterone does not inhibit the sodium-potassium ATPase in the proximal tubule. Its primary site of action is the collecting duct, where it enhances the activity of the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) and potassium channels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aldosterone does not significantly increase GFR. The hypokalemia is caused by direct stimulation of potassium secretion in the collecting duct, not by changes in filtration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-2 adrenergic receptor-mediated intracellular potassium shift is a mechanism of catecholamine-induced hypokalemia, not aldosterone-mediated hypokalemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aldosterone does not cause hypokalemia through impaired intestinal absorption. Its primary mechanism of potassium wasting is through increased renal excretion.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is found to have persistent blood pressure readings above the 95th percentile for age, sex, and height on three consecutive clinic visits. Physical examination reveals ambiguous genitalia. Laboratory studies show elevated serum 11-deoxycortisol, hypokalemia, and suppressed renin activity. Which of the following enzyme deficiencies is most likely responsible for this patient's hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 21-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency

choices.label: B
choices.text: 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deficiency

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aromatase deficiency

choices.label: E
choices.text: 5-alpha-reductase deficiency

explanation.summary: This child has congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency, which causes accumulation of 11-deoxycortisol and mineralocorticoids, leading to hypertension with hypokalemia. This is one of the forms of CAH that presents with hypertension in children.
explanation.whyCorrect: 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency is a form of congenital adrenal hyperplasia that causes accumulation of 11-deoxycorticosterone (a mineralocorticoid) and 11-deoxycortisol. The excess mineralocorticoids cause sodium retention and potassium loss, leading to hypertension and hypokalemia in children.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 21-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency is the most common form of CAH but typically presents with salt-wasting and hypotension (not hypertension) because it impairs both cortisol and aldosterone synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 3-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase deficiency impairs synthesis of all adrenal steroids and typically does not cause hypertension or excess mineralocorticoid activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aromatase deficiency affects estrogen synthesis but does not lead to mineralocorticoid excess or hypertension in children.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 5-alpha-reductase deficiency affects peripheral conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone and does not cause hypertension or mineralocorticoid excess.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis reports taking ibuprofen 800 mg three times daily for the past 6 months. Her blood pressure, which was previously well-controlled at 128/78 mm Hg on lisinopril, is now 154/94 mm Hg. Serum electrolytes and renal function are within normal limits. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which her current medication regimen contributed to the rise in blood pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NSAIDs inhibit COX-2 in the kidneys, decreasing vasodilating prostaglandins and activating RAAS

choices.label: B
choices.text: NSAIDs stimulate hepatic angiotensinogen synthesis through an estrogen-dependent pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: NSAIDs directly activate alpha-adrenergic receptors on vascular smooth muscle cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: NSAIDs inhibit nitric oxide synthase, reducing endothelial vasodilator production

choices.label: E
choices.text: NSAIDs increase sympathetic outflow from the central nervous system by crossing the blood-brain barrier
